body_txt: Everyone grieves in different ways. Some people take only months to work through the Five Stages of Grief , and some need years... or longer . But it's generally accepted that the best thing someone can do when grieving someone is to remember them with love, but eventually accept their passing and move on. Some people, however, don't do that. This trope is what happens when a character grieves someone for so much and so hard that their psyche breaks down and makes them irrational... or dangerous. They may become violent or Ax-Crazy . They may start to hallucinate that their loved one is still there (or see Bloody Hallucinations of Guilt if they blame themselves for the death), or that they didn't pass on and are still out there somewhere, or even that someone else is their lost loved one. They may be able to hide their collapsing psyche with a Mask of Sanity , but this will break sooner or later. In extremely creepy circumstances, they may even keep their loved one's corpse , perhaps moving it around as if they're still alive . Expect at least one Anguished Outburst . How long it takes for them to go crazy can vary from character to character (sometimes depending on what their mental health was like before the tragedy). Characters that often experience this trope are parents who have lost their child or a hero who lost their love . It could also be a result of Survivor Guilt , or because they Never Got to Say Goodbye . Note that this trope is not simply a character's mental health declining out of grief, but them actually suffering from a form of severe mental illness or insanity. Sub-trope of Sanity Slippage . Compare Excessive Mourning , Eternal Grief at the End , Freak Out , and Went Crazy When They Left . Contrast with Love Makes You Saner , To Absent Friends , and Moving Beyond Bereavement . Sometimes overlaps with Love Makes You Evil and Love Makes You Crazy . For when the griever tries to find a way to replace their lost love, see Replacement Goldfish . For when the griever has a reason to believe the dead character is still alive, see El Cid Ploy . Seeing as how a person having a breakdown due to grief is a very personal and possibly triggering thing for some people, no real-life examples, please!
